perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information that is disclosed verbally will also be included as proprietary. 2. Unless expressly authorized in writing by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not copy or disclose the Confidential Information to or use the Confidenti al Information for the benefit of any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information that is disclosed verbally will also be included as proprietary. 2. The Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not copy or disclose the Confidential Information to or use the Confidenti al Information for the benefit of any third party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Unless expressly authorized in writing by the Disclosing Party' allows the receiving party to copy and/or disclose the confidential information. This contradicts Arkansas Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arkansas Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctions to prevent the disclosure of trade secrets. By removing the Disclosing Party's control over disclosure in writing, the agreement potentially violates the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Arkansas Code § 4-75-604 (2023) - Injunctive relief :: 2023 Arkansas Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Arkansas Code 2023 Arkansas Code (here) 2020 Arkansas Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here Arkansas Code of 1987 (2023) Title 4 - BUSINESS AND COMMERCIAL LAW (§§ 4-1-101 — 4-119-105) Subtitle 6 - BUSINESS PRACTICES (§§ 4-70-101 — 4-76-108) Chapter 75 - UNFAIR PRACTICES (§§ 4-75-101 — 4-75-1404) Subchapter 6 - THEFT OF TRADE SECRETS (§§ 4-75-601 — 4-75-607) Section 4-75-604 - Injunctive relief Universal Citation: AR Code § 4-75-604 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. (b) Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist; however, the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (c) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (d) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Acts 1981, No. 439, § 2; A.S.A. 1947, § 70-1002. perturbation.justification: The original contract requires written authorization from the Disclosing Party before the Receiving Party can copy or disclose Confidential Information. The changed text removes this requirement, enabling the Receiving Party to copy or disclose confidential information without explicit written consent. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the removal of the Disclosing Party's control over disclosure in writing potentially violates the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, AR Code § 4-75-604 (a), states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined', supporting the idea that the original clause was there to prevent such misappropriation, and its removal creates a contradiction with the intention of this law. The removal weakens the protection against misappropriation of trade secrets as protected by Arkansas law.

perturbation.original_text: Before receiving any part of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party’s employees shall be required to read this Non -disclosure Agreement and, by receiving such Confidential Information, such employee shall acknowledge and agree to abide by the Receiving Party’s obligations hereunder. 3. Notwithstanding any other provisions of the Agreement, each party acknowledges that Confidential Information shall not include any information which: a. was known to the Receivi ng Party prior to the disclosure hereunder; b. was received from a third party not under an obligation of confidence to Receiving Party; c. is in the public domain at the time of disclosure hereunder or subsequently entered in the public domain without the fault of the Receiving Party; d. has been independently developed by an employee of the Receiving Party that has not had access directly or indirectly to Confidential Information, and Receiving Party can substantiate any claim of independent development by writt en evidence; or e. is required to be disclosed by law, provided however that the Receiving Party shall give immediate notice of any such request for disclosure and cooperate with the Disclosing Party in its efforts to obtain a protective order or other protec tion from the requirement or consequences of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Before receiving any part of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party’s employees shall be required to read this Non -disclosure Agreement and, by receiving such Confidential Information, such employee shall acknowledge and agree to abide by the Receiving Party’s obligations hereunder. 3. Notwithstanding any other provisions of the Agreement, each party acknowledges that Confidential Information shall not include any information which: a. was known to the Receivi ng Party prior to the disclosure hereunder; b. was received from a third party not under an obligation of confidence to Receiving Party; c. is in the public domain at the time of disclosure hereunder or subsequently entered in the public domain without the fault of the Receiving Party; d. has been independently developed by an employee of the Receiving Party that has not had access directly or indirectly to Confidential Information, and Receiving Party can substantiate any claim of independent development by writt en evidence; or e. is required to be disclosed by law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to give notice of any required disclosure by law, it prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking a protective order. This can conflict with the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act's provision for protecting trade secrets from disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Arkansas Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets. Removing the notice requirement hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their trade secrets through legal means (e.g., protective order) when disclosure is legally compelled, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the act.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the notice requirement in the changed text directly undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to seek injunctive relief under the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Arkansas Code § 4-75-604 (2023), explicitly states that "(a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to give notice of a legally compelled disclosure, the Disclosing Party is prevented from taking action to prevent misappropriation through seeking an injunction, creating a legal contradiction. The original text allowed for cooperation and protective measures, whereas the changed text eliminates this possibility, directly contradicting the ability to seek injunctive relief to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall agree upon the request of the Disclosing Party to return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information and supporting documentation provided to the Receiving Party. One copy of such documentation shall be retained by Receiving Party for archival/legal purposes. 6. No license, express or implied, in the Confidential Information is granted to either party other than to use the information in the manner and to the extent authorized by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall agree upon the request of the Disclosing Party to return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information and supporting documentation provided to the Receiving Party. 6. No license, express or implied, in the Confidential Information is granted to either party other than to use the information in the manner and to the extent authorized by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the Receiving Party's allowance to keep a copy of Confidential Information after termination of the agreement. While the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act does not specifically mention keeping a copy, this is generally accepted. However, more problematic is the implication that this agreement will be enforced, even without that. Arkansas Statute 4-75-607 limits the duration and scope to what is reasonably necessary to protect the trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: This omission could be considered legally risky because, depending on the nature of the confidential information, this could go outside the reasonable period necessary to protect that information. Furthermore, it has removed the Receiving Party's protection that they keep a copy for archival reasons.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the Receiving Party to retain a copy of Confidential Information for archival/legal purposes, while the modified contract removes this provision. The Perturbation Explanation highlights Arkansas Statute 4-75-607, which limits the duration and scope of trade secret protection to what is reasonably necessary. Although neither scraped snippet explicitly addresses retaining a copy, the Law Explanation posits that removing this allowance could be legally risky, potentially exceeding the reasonable protection period. The omission directly impacts the balance between protecting the Disclosing Party's confidential information and the Receiving Party's operational needs, creating a potential legal contradiction where the agreement's enforceability is called into question given the trade secret's protection duration and scope.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed, and interpreted by the laws of the State of Arkansas without reference to its choice of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed, and interpreted by the laws of the State of Arkansas.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'without reference to its choice of laws principles' could potentially lead to the application of another jurisdiction's laws if Arkansas's choice of law principles dictate that another state's law should apply. This is a problem for consumers because some states have laws protecting trade secrets, while others are more weak, or consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arkansas has enacted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets. By allowing for the possibility of another state's laws to govern, the protections offered by the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act could be undermined, potentially harming the disclosing party and contradicting the intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 4-75-601 - Definitions. :: 2010 Arkansas Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Arkansas Code 2024 Arkansas Code 2023 Arkansas Code 2020 Arkansas Code 2010 Arkansas Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Arkansas Code Title 4 - Business and Commercial Law Subtitle 6 - Business Practices Chapter 75 - Unfair Practices Subchapter 6 - Theft of Trade Secrets § 4-75-601 - Definitions. 4-75-601. Definitions. As used in this subchapter, unless the context otherwise requires: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (A) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (B) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (a) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (b) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (c) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity; (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (A) Der

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that Arkansas law governs the agreement 'without reference to its choice of laws principles.' By removing this phrase, the changed text allows for the possibility that Arkansas's choice of law principles might dictate that another state's laws should apply. The Law Explanation states this could undermine the protections offered by the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act, contradicting the intent of the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' and outlines protections for trade secrets under Arkansas law (4-75-601). By opening the door for another state's laws to govern, the modification weakens the application of Arkansas's trade secret protections, creating a legal loophole and thus a contradiction.
